title: Kleftiko
subtitle: Slow-roasted lamb with lemon, garlic, and herbs
excerpt: Kleftiko is a Greek method of slow-roasting lamb wrapped tightly in parchment so it steams in its own juices. The meat becomes very tender, falling from the bone, with a concentrated lemon and herb flavour. It needs a long time in a low oven but very little attention.
sourceNotes: The name kleftiko refers to the klephts, Greek mountain rebels who are said to have slow-cooked stolen lamb in covered pits to avoid detection from the smoke. The domestic version wraps the lamb in paper and cooks it in a low oven. A staple of Greek Sunday tables and festive meals.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Marinate the lamb overnight for the best flavour.

glossaryTerms.definition: A cooking method where food is wrapped tightly in parchment or foil so it steams in its own juices, concentrating the flavours inside the parcel.

content.text: , sealed inside layers of parchment so it steams gently in its own juices for three and a half hours. The lamb becomes very tender and pulls easily from the bone, carrying the flavours of lemon, garlic, and herbs through every bite.
The overnight marinade is not strictly required but makes a real difference. If you're short on time, an hour at room temperature in the marinade still helps.

items.prepNote: bone-in, scored deeply with a knife
half sliced for inserting, half crushed for marinade

content.text: You need enough parchment paper to wrap the lamb generously, and foil as a second layer. A deep roasting tin holds everything in place.
Score the lamb all over deeply with a sharp knife, about 2 cm cuts. Push sliced garlic cloves into the cuts.

Mix the crushed garlic, lemon zest, lemon juice, olive oil, and dried oregano together. Rub this marinade all over the lamb. Tuck in the thyme and rosemary sprigs. Cover and refrigerate overnight, or for at least 1 hour.

Heat the oven to 160°C. Lay a large sheet of parchment in a deep roasting tin. If using, scatter the potato chunks and tomato quarters in the centre. Place the lamb on top. Bring up the sides of the parchment and scrunch it to seal. Wrap the whole parcel in foil. The seal must be tight so no steam escapes.
Roast for 3 hours 30 minutes. The lamb should pull apart easily with two forks. If it doesn't, seal it back up and return it for another 30 minutes.
Open the parcel carefully at the table or in the kitchen. The steam inside is very hot. Serve the lamb directly from the parchment with the cooking juices poured over.

items.symptom: The lamb is not tender after 3 hours 30 minutes
items.cause: The parcel was not sealed tightly and steam escaped
items.fix: Seal it with a second layer of foil and return to the oven for another 45 minutes. A tight seal is the most important part of the method.

items.symptom: The potatoes are not cooked through
items.cause: Potatoes cut too large, or too far from the lamb
items.fix: Cut potatoes into chunks about 3 cm; larger pieces may not cook through in the given time. Place them directly under and around the lamb so they catch the juices.
Overnight marinating is the ideal. The lamb carries the lemon and garlic much deeper when it has had time to absorb the marinade.

content.text: Kleftiko is a dish associated with Greek Easter and Sunday celebrations. In Cypriot cooking it is made in communal outdoor ovens called foukou. At home the oven version gives much the same result. It is a dish that needs almost nothing alongside it, just the cooking juices and some bread to mop them up.
